title: Rags to Riches
backstory: Your Sim arrives in town with the clothes on their back, a patch of bare dirt, and not a single Simoleon to their name. No job will save them — only their own two hands. This is the challenge that started a thousand simmers actually using the painting easel, and it's the gateway drug to every harder run in this book.
bestFor: Players bored of starting rich; anyone learning the money skills
premise: Your Sim arrives in town with the clothes on their back, a patch of bare dirt, and not a single Simoleon to their name.
goal: Build your household funds up to **§100,000**, with every Simoleon earned from skills and odd jobs rather than a paid (rabbit-hole) career.
gettingStarted: Create one young adult Sim with any traits — though *Frugal*, *Hardworking*-style aspirations and anything that boosts a money skill will help. Move them onto the largest cheap empty lot you can find, then set up the bare start:

setup.label: Lot
setup.value: Empty, 20×15 or larger, cheapest you can find

setup.label: Sims
setup.value: One young adult; no starting furniture or shelter

generalRules: No money cheats after the §0 start. `bb.moveobjects` for building is fine.
Only buy what you can currently afford — no going into a negative balance via tricks.
No gifts, transfers, or money from any other household.
specificRules: No rabbit-hole career income. Money comes only from skills and self-employment — painting, writing, gardening, fishing, woodworking, crafting, collecting.
You begin with no walls and no roof; build your shelter up from open sky as you can afford it.
No moving into a pre-furnished house to skip the early grind.
strategyTips: *Fishing and collecting* are your day-one lifeline — they cost nothing and the frogs/crystals/fish sell immediately. Fish near the starting pond while your other skills build.
*The painting easel* is the classic engine: buy the cheapest one the moment you can, and paint constantly. Painting skill raises the value of every canvas, so early grind compounds fast.
Plant anything you find foraging — a free garden is passive income that grows while you sleep.
wantItHarder: *One-crop start:* every Simoleon must trace back to a single starting plant before you may diversify.
*Off-grid:* enable the Off-the-Grid lot trait so you have no power or water to lean on either.
variations: Run it as a *family* rags-to-riches (start a couple with kids at §0), or set the target to §250,000 for a full-lifetime version. ---
scoring: Race friends by *in-game days to §100,000*: under 40 days is strong, under 25 is expert. Lower is better.
credit: A community staple adapted from the long-running Rags to Riches format that predates The Sims 4.
allowed: Use `bb.moveobjects` for building and placement.
Earn from any self-employment: painting, writing, gardening, fishing, woodworking, flower arranging, programming/freelance, or collecting.
Forage and eat wild plants; sell anything your Sim makes or finds.
Take freelance/odd-job gigs (these aren't salaried rabbit-hole careers).
notAllowed: No money cheats after the §0 start, and no negative-balance tricks.
No rabbit-hole salaried careers (the regular job panel).
No gifts, transfers, or money from any other household.
No moving into a pre-built or pre-furnished home, and no selling gifted/cheated items.
objectives: Start at §0 on a bare lot
Earn your first §1,000 from a skill or odd job
Build a one-room shelter (bed, toilet, shower, fridge)
Reach level 7 in a money-making skill
baseGameVersion: Already fully base-game. Without Seasons you simply lose the weather stakes, and without Eco Lifestyle you skip off-grid options — painting, fishing, gardening and woodworking still cover every Simoleon.

title: From Tenant to Tycoon
backstory: You start the month scraping together rent for a single cramped unit. You end it as the landlord every other tenant answers to. This is the rags-to-riches climb reimagined for the For Rent era — a slow, satisfying rise from paying rent to collecting it.
bestFor: Players who love management and property over the painting grind
premise: You start the month scraping together rent for a single cramped unit.
goal: Go from renting a single unit you can barely afford to **owning and running three profitable rental properties**.
gettingStarted: Create one ambitious young adult. Rather than buying a home, rent the cheapest available unit in a residential rental lot. Set funds low so the early squeeze is real:

setup.label: Lot
setup.value: Rent the cheapest unit you can find

setup.label: Sims
setup.value: One tenant; a *Business Savvy* aspiration suits

generalRules: No money cheats after the §800 start.
Every Simoleon is earned — no transfers or gifts.
You must live as a tenant first; you can't buy a rental until you've paid rent honestly.
specificRules: Pay your own rent on time, every cycle. Eviction is a fail.
Save toward your first rentable property; buy it only when you can afford it outright.
Every property you own must stay genuinely livable — handle repairs, pests, mould and tenant complaints.
Reinvest profits into the next property before upgrading your own lifestyle.
strategyTips: Keep your own unit cheap far longer than feels comfortable — every Simoleon you don't spend on yourself is a deposit on your first building.
A small side income (handiness for your own repairs, a home-based skill) smooths the gap between buying a property and it turning profit.
Screen for the cheapest run-down building first; low purchase price beats a pretty one when you're starting out.
wantItHarder: *Slumlord to saint:* buy the most run-down building in town and renovate it to a five-star rental before buying a second.
Forbid any personal income — you may only ever earn through rent.
milestones: Pay your first month's rent on time
Keep a tenant happy for a full lease
variations: Play it as a *family business* where each grown child manages one building, or cap it at one perfect five-star property instead of three. ---
scoring: +1 point per fully-occupied rental month; aim for a long unbroken streak across all three buildings.
credit: Original — Plumbob, built around the For Rent property system.
allowed: Keep a modest personal side income (handiness repairs, a home skill) to bridge to profit.
Use `bb.moveobjects` for renovations.
Buy run-down or cheap buildings and renovate them up.
notAllowed: No money cheats after the §800 start.
No buying a rental until you've lived as a paying tenant and can afford it outright.
No transfers or gifts; don't upgrade your own lifestyle before reinvesting in the next property.
Missing your own rent (eviction) is an instant fail.
objectives: Pay your first month's rent on time
Buy your first rental property outright
Keep a tenant happy through a full lease
Own and run three profitable rentals
baseGameVersion: For Rent is required for the rental system, so there's no true base-game version. The closest base-game stand-in is a landlord *roleplay* — own several lots and track 'rent' yourself — but the real tenant/property mechanics need For Rent.

title: The One-Crop Empire
backstory: Everything you will ever own grows from a single seed. One plant, chosen at the start, becomes the root of an entire fortune — every grafted branch, every bottle of nectar, every Simoleon traceable back to that first humble sprout.
bestFor: Gardening lovers who want a purist's run
premise: Everything you will ever own grows from a single seed.
goal: Reach **§75,000** with every income stream rooted in one original plant and its descendants.
gettingStarted: Create one Sim with the *Freelance Botanist* aspiration or *Loves Outdoors* trait. Move onto an empty rural lot and set a small start:

setup.label: Lot
setup.value: Empty rural lot with growing space

generalRules: No money cheats after the §500 start.
All income is earned by your own household.
No transfers or gifts.
specificRules: Buy or forage exactly **one** plant or seed to begin. Everything you grow must descend from it via harvest or graft.
Income comes only from that plant's produce, its grafts, or goods you craft from it (nectar, arrangements, preserves).
No careers, gigs, or unrelated collectibles for money.
Reinvest only into gardening and processing equipment.
strategyTips: Choose your founding plant carefully: a plant that *grafts well* lets you branch into new produce while staying within the rules.
Push quality up — Excellent-quality produce sells for many times the base price, and quality climbs as your gardening skill does.
Nectar and flower arranging multiply the value of raw produce; treat them as your "processing industry."
wantItHarder: Pick the lowest-value common plant available as your founder and still reach the target.
Forbid grafting — the empire must run on one unmodified crop.
variations: Make it generational: the founding plant is an heirloom passed down, and each generation must add one new grafted branch. ---
scoring: Track *fewest distinct purchases of outside seeds* (ideally exactly one). A true single-plant empire scores highest.
credit: Original — Plumbob.
allowed: Graft, splice, fertilise and propagate freely from your single origin plant.
Sell produce, flower arrangements, and (with Horse Ranch) nectar made from your crop.
Use `bb.moveobjects`.
notAllowed: No income from any plant not descended from the origin plant.
No money cheats after setup; no rabbit-hole careers.
You may eat foraged plants but never sell them — only your lineage earns money.
objectives: Choose and plant your single origin crop
Harvest and sell your first batch
Graft or splice to expand the lineage
Add a value-add stream (flower arranging, or nectar with Horse Ranch) from the same crop
Reach §75,000 — all traceable to the one plant
baseGameVersion: Already base-game. Without Seasons, plant year-round; nectar needs Horse Ranch, so base-game players lean on flower arranging plus grafted produce to reach the target.

title: Self-Made Mogul
backstory: A shuttered storefront, a thousand Simoleons, and a dream. You'll sweat the floor yourself — stocking shelves, ringing up customers, charming the regulars — and claw your way from a one-person operation to a five-star institution.
bestFor: Players who want active management, not idle skilling
premise: A shuttered storefront, a thousand Simoleons, and a dream.
goal: Build a **five-star retail store or restaurant** starting from near-zero funds.
gettingStarted: Create one owner with the *Fabulously Wealthy* or *Master Chef* aspiration. Buy a tiny home and the cheapest commercial lot you can:

setup.label: Lot
setup.value: Small home + a shoestring business lot

setup.label: Sims
setup.value: One owner who works the floor

generalRules: No money cheats after the §1,000 start.
All household income comes from the business once it opens.
No outside transfers or gifts.
specificRules: The owner must work the business in person — no fully absentee management.
Reinvest profits into stock, staff and perks before personal luxuries.
Letting the business go bankrupt (forced closure) is a fail.
strategyTips: Open small. A handful of high-margin items beats a huge shop you can't keep stocked.
The *Retail/Gourmet perks* are the real progression — prioritise the ones that speed up customers and boost markup.
Set markup carefully: too high and customers walk, too low and you never bank perks. Watch the satisfaction icons and adjust.
wantItHarder: Run two businesses at once and get both to five stars before the owner reaches elder.
No hired staff — the owner does everything, every shift.
variations: Theme the business strictly (a bakery, a bookshop, a vampire-only bar) and only sell on-theme goods. ---
scoring: +1 per star reached; +5 for a perfect-rating week with no customer complaints.
credit: Original — Plumbob, built around the active retail/restaurant systems.
allowed: Run either a Get to Work retail store or a Dine Out restaurant — your choice.
Keep a small personal skill income to fund the opening.
Hire and manage employees, and use `bb.moveobjects`.
notAllowed: No money cheats — save the opening capital honestly.
No opening the business with cheated funds.
No perk-point cheats; earn perks through real operation.
objectives: Save the capital to open your business
Unlock key perks (cheaper stock/ingredients, faster service)
baseGameVersion: Requires Get to Work or Dine Out — there's no base-game storefront. Base-game stand-in: run a 'home business' selling crafted paintings/woodwork and treat a savings target as your '5-star', but the true challenge needs one of those packs.

title: Penny Pincher
backstory: Some people get rich by earning more. Your Sim gets rich by refusing to spend. For an entire lifetime, no single purchase may ever cross §50 — every wall, every meal, every stick of furniture bought in the cheapest possible increments.
bestFor: Players who love a quirky self-imposed rule
premise: Some people get rich by earning more.
goal: Reach **§50,000 net worth** without ever making a single purchase over §50.
gettingStarted: Create one thrifty Sim (*Frugal*-leaning traits suit). Move onto a cheap small lot:
generalRules: No money cheats after the §200 start.
Income from any honest source is allowed.
No gifts or transfers.
specificRules: No single transaction may exceed §50 — ever. This covers furniture, build items, food and bills paid where they can be paid in parts.
Build your whole home from sub-§50 pieces only.
strategyTips: Build with the cheapest wall and floor patterns, then decorate over time as your *skills* (not spending) raise your home's livability.
Cook at home from cheap ingredients; never order delivery.
Many "expensive" needs (fun, social) are free — parks, libraries, and gym equipment you slowly assemble piece by sub-§50 piece.
wantItHarder: Drop the cap to **§20** per purchase for the entire life.
Forbid selling anything too — wealth must come purely from banked earnings.
milestones: Furnish a starter room under the cap
variations: Family edition: the §50 cap applies to a household of four, making every grocery run a puzzle. ---
scoring: Track your *most expensive single purchase* across the whole run; bragging rights for the lowest ceiling actually used.
credit: Original — Plumbob.
allowed: Make as many purchases as you like, as long as each item costs §50 or less.
Use free, found, crafted or harvested items freely.
Use `bb.moveobjects`.
notAllowed: No single purchase over §50 — check the buy price of every object.
No money cheats.
No accepting expensive gifts or hand-me-downs to dodge the cap.
objectives: Furnish a starter home using only ≤§50 items
Build a functional kitchen and bathroom under the cap
Reach §50,000 net worth — never having bought a >§50 item
baseGameVersion: Already base-game. Tiny Living just adds cheap space-saving furniture; without it, build small and pick the cheapest swatches.

title: The Yard-Sale Hustle
backstory: Your Sim never makes anything and never holds a job. They just have an eye — for the frog in the log, the crystal in the dirt, the fossil nobody else dug up — and they turn that eye into a fortune, one flip at a time.
bestFor: Explorers who like collecting over grinding
premise: Your Sim never makes anything and never holds a job.
goal: Reach **§40,000** earned only from collecting and reselling found objects.
gettingStarted: Create one curious Sim (*The Curator* aspiration is a natural fit). A cheap small home is all you need:
generalRules: No money cheats after the §1,000 start.
Earn only with your own household.
No gifts or transfers.
specificRules: Income comes only from selling collectibles — frogs, crystals, metals, fossils, fish, space rocks, MySims trophies, postcards and the like.
No crafting careers or skill products sold for money.
strategyTips: *Crystals and metals* feed the rock-cutting machine route to higher value; even raw, rarer finds sell well.
Travel widely — each world and vacation destination has its own collectible tables.
Breeding frogs and completing collections unlocks rarer, pricier variants worth chasing.
wantItHarder: Complete a full collection (e.g. all crystals) before you're allowed to sell *any* duplicates.
Base-game collectibles only — no pack collections to pad your income.
variations: Run it as a travelling picker who must move worlds every time they hit §10,000. ---
scoring: +5 per completed collection; the run is "perfect" if you finish a collection before selling any duplicates.
credit: Original — Plumbob.
allowed: Collect anything: crystals, metals, fossils, frogs, fish, space rocks, postcards, MySims trophies, and more.
Sell collectibles raw, or upgrade/cut them first for more value.
Buy low and resell where you can.
notAllowed: No income from careers, skills-for-hire, or gardening — collecting and reselling only.
No money cheats.
No gifted items to sell.
objectives: Find a rare, high-value collectible (e.g., a cut geode crystal)
baseGameVersion: Already base-game. Jungle Adventure and Snowy Escape add more collectibles, but base-game crystals, metals, fossils, frogs and fish are plenty to reach the goal.

title: Debt to Diamonds
backstory: Your Sim doesn't start at zero — they start below it. A §50,000 millstone of debt hangs over them from day one, and only once it's gone may they even think about the mansion they're determined to build, brick by mortgage-free brick.
bestFor: Players who like a tracked, bill-driven goal
premise: Your Sim doesn't start at zero — they start below it.
goal: Clear a **§50,000 starting debt**, then build a mortgage-free mansion worth **§200,000+**.
gettingStarted: Create one earner. Move into a modest home, set funds to zero, and write your §50,000 debt on a tracker (a notes app or the in-game notebook):
generalRules: No money cheats after the §0 start.
All income earned by your household.
No gifts or transfers.
specificRules: Pay a fixed weekly amount toward the tracked debt — treat it like a real bill that must be paid first.
No luxury purchases until the debt tracker reads zero.
Dropping below your agreed minimum balance is a fail.
strategyTips: Set a realistic weekly payment you can always meet; consistency matters more than big irregular lumps.
A stable home-based income (writing royalties, a maxed painting skill) makes the weekly bill painless.
Only *after* zero do you pivot hard into the mansion build — keep the two phases mentally separate.
wantItHarder: Add interest: the debt grows 5% each week you don't make your full payment.
Double the debt to §100,000.
variations: Frame it as medical or business debt with a roleplay backstory that dictates how your Sim is allowed to earn. ---
scoring: Track *weeks to clear the debt*; fewer is better. Bonus for never missing a weekly payment.
credit: Original — Plumbob.
allowed: Use any legitimate income to repay the debt (skills, self-employment, and — your call — careers).
Use `bb.moveobjects` for the mansion build.
Track the §50,000 debt yourself, since Sims can't hold a negative balance: start at §0 and bank §50,000 before you spend anything on luxuries.
notAllowed: No money cheats; the §50,000 must be honestly 'repaid' before any luxury spending.
No new luxury purchases until the debt line is cleared.
No transfers or gifts.
objectives: Set your tracked §50,000 debt and start at §0
baseGameVersion: Already base-game. Get to Work and Seasons just add income variety and stakes; the debt-and-build loop is entirely base-game.

title: Off-the-Grid Tycoon
backstory: No power lines. No water mains. No safety net. Your Sim is going to get genuinely, mansion-and-all rich — while every drop of water is hauled and every watt is caught from the sun. Comfort here isn't bought; it's engineered.
bestFor: Survival fans who also want the wealth climb
premise: No power lines.
goal: Reach **§150,000** on a permanently off-the-grid lot.
gettingStarted: Create one self-reliant Sim. Move onto a lot and apply the **Off-the-Grid** lot trait in build mode:
In Build Mode → Lot Traits → add **Off-the-Grid**

setup.label: Lot
setup.value: Any lot with the Off-the-Grid trait enabled

generalRules: No money cheats after the §1,000 start.
Income earned by your household only.
No gifts or transfers.
specificRules: The lot stays Off-the-Grid for the entire challenge.
Power comes only from solar panels, generators you build, or candles; water from wells, dew collectors and rain.
Wealth may come from any honest source, but all comfort must be self-generated.
strategyTips: Solar panels and the *Eco Lifestyle* dew collectors pay for themselves fast — prioritise them over décor early.
Wood-fired stoves and fireplaces cover cooking and warmth without the grid.
Gardening and a fishing pond make food free, freeing your income entirely for the wealth goal.
wantItHarder: Reach the target without buying a single power-generating object — only found or crafted solutions.
Combine with Off-the-Grid *and* a §0 start.
variations: Set it on a remote Sulani or Henford lot for a homestead flavour, or run it as an off-grid family. ---
scoring: Reach the target while keeping your lot's eco-footprint *green* the whole way for a perfect run.
credit: Original — Plumbob.
allowed: Use solar panels, dew collectors, candles, fireplaces and wells for power and water.
Earn from any self-employment.
Use `bb.moveobjects`.
notAllowed: The Off-the-Grid lot trait stays on for the entire challenge — never remove it.
No connecting to power, water or city services.
No money cheats.
objectives: Set the lot Off-the-Grid and start
Survive your first week with no power or water
baseGameVersion: The Off-the-Grid lot trait is free/base-game. Without Eco Lifestyle, Island Living or Cottage Living you rely on base-game candles, fireplaces, fishing and gardening; the pack power-generators are optional comforts.

title: Garden of Gold
backstory: Dirt under the fingernails, a fortune in the flowerbeds. Your Sim builds an empire out of petals, produce and patiently-aged nectar — proof that the richest thing on the lot can be the garden itself.
bestFor: Gardening devotees
premise: Dirt under the fingernails, a fortune in the flowerbeds.
goal: Reach **§100,000** from gardening, floristry and nectar alone.
gettingStarted: Create one green-thumbed Sim (*Freelance Botanist* aspiration). Move to a lot with room to grow:
generalRules: No money cheats after the §1,500 start.
Earn only with your household.
No gifts or transfers.
specificRules: Income only from produce, flower arrangements and nectar.
No careers or unrelated crafts.
Improve plant quality over time through care and grafting.
strategyTips: *Flower arranging* turns cheap blooms into high-value bouquets — a fast early earner while crops mature.
Aged nectar appreciates dramatically; start a cellar early and let bottles sit.
Seasons lets you force-bloom and manage temperature, smoothing out winter income gaps.
wantItHarder: Reach the target growing only within one greenhouse footprint — no expanding the garden's land area.
Nectar-only: every Simoleon must come from the nectar trade.
milestones: First nectar batch aged and sold
variations: Run it as a family flower shop, or pair with a roadside stall roleplay where you "sell" at weekly markets. ---
scoring: +1 per plant species at Excellent quality; a fully Excellent garden is a perfect run.
credit: Original — Plumbob.
allowed: Earn from gardening, flower arranging, and (with Horse Ranch) nectar.
Sell produce, bouquets and arrangements.
Use `bb.moveobjects`.
notAllowed: No income outside gardening, floristry and nectar.
No money cheats; no rabbit-hole careers.
objectives: Produce sellable nectar (or, base-game, scale up floristry)
baseGameVersion: Gardening and flower arranging are base-game; nectar needs Horse Ranch, so base-game players reach the target on produce and arrangements alone.

title: The Side-Hustle Stack
backstory: No job, no problem — your Sim has five. A painter by morning, a writer by lunch, a gardener by afternoon, a freelancer by evening, a collector on the weekend. The challenge isn't earning; it's juggling, and never letting a single plate stop spinning.
bestFor: Players who like maximising every Sim-hour
premise: No job, no problem — your Sim has five.
goal: Run **five distinct income streams simultaneously** and reach **§80,000**.
gettingStarted: Create one high-energy Sim. A small home-studio is ideal:
generalRules: No money cheats after the §1,000 start.
Earn with your own household only.
No gifts or transfers.
specificRules: No rabbit-hole salaried career.
Maintain five different active income streams (e.g. painting, writing, gardening, freelance, collecting). Each must produce real income each week.
Dropping below five active streams for a full week is a fail.
strategyTips: Pick streams that run on *different need-timers* — gardening and nectar work while you sleep; painting and writing fill your waking hours.
Batch similar tasks: harvest and replant in one trip, then paint until energy runs low.
*Wellness/meditation* and a tidy needs setup buy you the extra hours the fifth stream demands.
wantItHarder: Push to **seven** simultaneous streams and hold them for a full season.
No passive streams — every stream must require active daily input.
variations: Spread the streams across a *household* (one Sim per hustle) for a "family of freelancers" version. ---
scoring: +1 per simultaneous stream beyond five; hold the maximum for a full season for a top score.
credit: Original — Plumbob.
allowed: Run any five distinct self-employment streams at once (painting, writing, gardening, fishing, woodworking, programming/freelance, collecting, a home business…).
Use `bb.moveobjects`.
notAllowed: No single stream may provide more than about half your income — keep all five genuinely active.
No money cheats.
No salaried rabbit-hole career as one of the five (self-employment only).
objectives: Run all five streams in the same week
baseGameVersion: Already base-game. City Living's Freelancer is a handy stream, but base-game self-employment easily covers all five.

title: Homeless to Homeowner
backstory: A park bench. A pocket with nothing in it. A long, cold road to a front door of your own. This is rags-to-riches stripped to the bone — no lot, no shelter, no skills, just a Sim and the will to climb off the street.
bestFor: Players who want the hardest possible start
premise: A park bench.
goal: Go from homeless with no shelter to owning a **fully furnished home worth §60,000+**.
gettingStarted: Create one Sim with nothing. Move onto a public-feeling or empty lot with no build, and zero out funds:
generalRules: No money cheats after the §0 start.
Earn with your own household only.
No gifts or transfers.
specificRules: Begin with no bed, no walls and no roof. Sleep on public benches, eat from the cheapest sources, wash in gym/public bathrooms.
Build up only from earned income, one item at a time.
Buying or moving into a home before you've earned it is a fail.
strategyTips: Public spaces are your early lifeline: gyms for showers, libraries for skill-building and computers, parks for free food events.
Collecting and busking-style skills (guitar, painting on a cheap easel once you can afford one) get the first Simoleons flowing.
Bank toward a single bed and a tent/roof before anything else — restored energy multiplies everything else you can do.
wantItHarder: Spend your first full week with no built shelter at all before you may place a single wall.
Winter-start in Seasons, so exposure is genuinely dangerous.
variations: The *Homeless Family* version: a parent and child must survive the street together. ---
scoring: Track *days spent fully homeless* before placing your first wall; a longer street phase is a harder, higher-scoring run.
credit: A community survival staple (the Homeless Challenge), expanded here with a homeowner goal.
allowed: Sleep on park benches and use public toilets, showers and gyms while homeless.
Eat cheaply at public venues; earn from any self-employment.
Use `bb.moveobjects` once you're building.
notAllowed: No owned lot or shelter at the start — truly homeless.
No money cheats; no gifts or transfers.
No moving straight into a furnished home.
objectives: Earn a steady income while homeless
Save the down payment for a lot
Reach a fully furnished home worth §60,000+
baseGameVersion: Already base-game. City Living apartments and Seasons weather add flavour and stakes but aren't required — public venues for needs are all base-game.

title: Single Simoleon Start
backstory: One Simoleon. That's the whole inheritance. From that single coin — and never a cheat again — your Sim must build a life worth fifty thousand of them.
bestFor: Purists who want a clean, brutal number to start from
premise: One Simoleon.
goal: Reach **§50,000** having started with a single Simoleon.
gettingStarted: Create one Sim, move onto an empty small lot, and set funds to exactly one:
generalRules: No money cheats after the §1 start.
Earn with your own household only.
No gifts or transfers.
specificRules: Your entire life is built upward from one Simoleon.
Earn through any honest skill or gig.
Buy only what you can afford in the moment.
strategyTips: With §1 you can't buy anything useful — your first earnings must come from *free* actions: foraging, fishing with a found rod opportunity, or social events with free food.
The jump from §1 to your first easel is the hardest stretch; collecting bridges it fastest.
Once you can afford one money-skill object, tunnel into it before diversifying.
wantItHarder: No selling of starter or found items — only money you actively earn through skills counts toward growth.
Raise the target to §150,000 for a lifetime version.
variations: Combine with Off-the-Grid for a "one coin, no grid" purist run. ---
scoring: *Days to escape single digits* (reach §100); a fast escape from the §1 trap is the real flex.
credit: Original — Plumbob, in the spirit of extreme-start Rags to Riches runs.
allowed: Start with exactly §1 (`money 1`).
Earn from any self-employment.
Use `bb.moveobjects`.
notAllowed: No money cheats after the §1 start.
No gifts or transfers.
No rabbit-hole salaried careers (purist version).
objectives: Build basic shelter and cover needs
baseGameVersion: Already base-game. Any skill-friendly pack helps but nothing is required.

title: Freelance Famine
backstory: No salary. No safety. No steady paycheque ever lands in your Sim's account. They live gig to gig, feast to famine, riding the highs of a big commission and white-knuckling the weeks with nothing — for an entire lifetime.
bestFor: Players who want cash-flow tension over a slow grind
premise: No salary.
goal: Support a Sim for a full lifetime and reach **§60,000** using only freelance and gig income.
gettingStarted: Create one Sim and join the **Freelancer** career (or live on gig work and self-employment if you lack City Living). Modest starting funds:
generalRules: No money cheats after the §1,000 start.
Earn with your own household only.
No gifts or transfers.
specificRules: Income only from the Freelancer career and odd gigs/self-employment.
No salaried rabbit-hole job, ever.
You must ride out the lean weeks — no taking a stable job to smooth them.
strategyTips: Build a cash buffer in good weeks so a dry spell can't sink you — the famine is the whole point, but a cushion keeps you alive.
Specialise: a single high-level freelance skill earns far more per gig than spreading thin.
Keep bills low and a cheap food supply growing so a zero-income week costs you nothing fatal.
wantItHarder: Impose a self-declared "dry month" each season where you bank no new gigs and must live on savings.
No buffer allowed — you must always spend down below §500 before each new week.
variations: The *struggling-artist* version: only creative freelance gigs count, and your Sim must also chase fame. ---
scoring: +1 per consecutive week you finish in the black; the longest unbroken solvent streak wins.
credit: Original — Plumbob.
allowed: Use the City Living Freelancer career (Writer, Programmer, Artist or Maker) plus odd-job gigs.
Sell your freelance output for extra income.
notAllowed: No salaried rabbit-hole careers — freelance and gig income only.
No money cheats; no money from other households.
objectives: Complete a gig before its deadline
Survive a 'dry spell' (a week with no gig) without going broke
Reach §60,000 across a full lifetime — freelance only
baseGameVersion: The Freelancer career needs City Living. Base-game stand-in: combine phone 'odd jobs' with self-employment (painting/writing/programming sold by the piece) as your freelance income to reach §60,000.

title: The Legacy Challenge
backstory: The one that started it all. A single founder steps onto a bare lot with nothing but the clothes they're wearing, and ten generations later their descendants still live on that exact spot — a family tree grown from dirt into a dynasty. It's less a challenge than a way of life, and almost every other challenge in this category is its child.
bestFor: Players who want a long-haul, multi-generational anchor save
premise: The one that started it all.
goal: Take a single bloodline through **ten unbroken generations**, always living on the founder's original lot.
gettingStarted: Create one young-adult founder with any traits and aspiration. Move them onto a large empty lot (the classic is a 50×50), and play with no money cheats from that point on.

setup.label: Lot
setup.value: Largest empty lot you can find (50×50 classic)

setup.label: Funds
setup.value: Starting funds only; never topped up by cheats

generalRules: No money cheats, ever, after setup.
The family never moves — every generation lives and dies on the founding lot.
Each generation has exactly one named heir who inherits the lot and bloodline.
specificRules: Heirs are produced according to your chosen succession laws — no swapping laws mid-run to suit you.
Only the current heir's children can continue the line.
The line must survive ten full generations; an heir dying with no successor ends the legacy.
strategyTips: Always have the *next* heir secured before an heir reaches elder — a sudden death with no successor is the classic legacy-ender.
Parenthood's character values make raising capable heirs far easier; lean on it.
Build vertically and in phases — you'll be adding rooms for a growing family for ten generations, so leave space.
wantItHarder: Pick the strictest succession laws (Strict Traditional bloodline + Strict heir law) and never break them, even when the "correct" heir is your worst Sim.
Combine with a §0, off-grid founder for a true from-nothing dynasty.
milestones: Establish the founder and choose your laws
variations: Theme the dynasty (a family of artists, of villains, of occults), or layer Not So Berry's colour rules on top for a themed legacy. ---
scoring: The Legacy Challenge has a long-standing optional points system. Award points across categories as the family achieves them: each **generation** completed, **aspirations** completed, **skills** maxed, **careers** topped, **collections** finished, and special feats (e.g. a member reaching the top of a difficult skill or hosting a perfect event). Tally your dynasty's lifetime score at Generation 10. Treat it as a personal high score to beat on your next run.
credit: The Legacy Challenge was created by **Pinstar**, carried over from earlier Sims games and maintained for The Sims 4; the succession-law framework is the community standard.
allowed: Use `bb.moveobjects`, earned aspiration reward traits, and Potions of Youth.
Continue the line by birth or adoption.
Choose your succession laws at the start — gender, bloodline, heir and species — and play to them.
notAllowed: No money cheats after the §1,800 founding start.
Never move off the founder's original lot — the whole legacy lives there.
The heir must be born or adopted in-game, never created in CAS, and no marrying in outside fortunes to skip the early grind.
objectives: Found a Sim on an empty lot with §1,800
Choose your succession laws (gender, bloodline, heir, species)
Produce a Gen 2 heir who meets your laws
Crown the Gen 10 heir on the original lot
baseGameVersion: Fully base-game. Pinstar's optional scoring — points for each generation plus maxed skills, topped careers, completed aspirations and painted portraits — needs no DLC.

title: The 100 Baby Challenge
backstory: One mother. One hundred children. As few generations as humanly (or in-game-ly) possible. It's the most infamous challenge in the game for a reason: an endurance test of nappies, donors and sheer logistics where the matriarch does everything herself and the babies never stop coming.
bestFor: Players who want a marathon and love chaos
premise: One mother.
goal: Give birth to **100 children** using as **few matriarchs as possible**. The run ends the instant the 100th child is born — or the instant you break a rule.
gettingStarted: Create one young-adult matriarch who can get pregnant (any gender customisation that allows pregnancy; any traits/aspiration). Move her into any home she can afford. Turn **NPC auto-aging on** so the town keeps generating fresh donors.

setup.label: Lot
setup.value: Any home affordable on starting funds; move later only if affordable

generalRules: **No cheats** except `bb.moveobjects` (building) and resolving glitches. No money cheats; no mods that raise household size or pay child support.
Aging on, normal lifespan.
Raise every child properly — you may never make room by neglect, death, or letting Social Services take a child.
specificRules: **One donor, one child.** A Sim who has fathered one of your children can never be used again — every child has a different father. Donors give **no** help: they don't move in, mind children, or provide money.
The matriarch may only work a **work-from-home / freelance / self-employed** job — no rabbit-hole career, no part-time. Teens may take part-time jobs to help.
**No nanny or babysitter, ever.** Maids, gardeners and repair techs are allowed but must not do childcare; a butler must have childcare disabled.
Children's traits are set by the **in-game randomiser** (you may pick aspirations to match). You may not influence a baby's gender.
The matriarch can't marry while of child-bearing age; she may marry as an elder but must move out with her spouse.

**Aging-up gates** (no rushing): newborns only on their birthday notification; toddlers at level 3 in all toddler skills; children/teens only with an A in school (plus a character value if you have Parenthood). Household holds a maximum of **seven children at a time**.

**Succession:** when the matriarch becomes an elder and can no longer bear children, her **youngest daughter** becomes the next matriarch (you may move the elder out once that daughter is a young adult).
strategyTips: The matriarch's income lifeline is selling **paintings, books or garden produce** from home — set one up early, because she literally cannot hold a normal job.

Chase **multiples**: the *Fertile* reward trait, the *On a Ley Line* lot trait, and the Wellness "Fertility Massage" all raise your odds of twins and triplets — each multiple birth is two or three babies toward 100 from a single pregnancy, and fewer pregnancies means fewer matriarchs.
Keep a written list of used donors so you never accidentally repeat one (an instant rule-break).
wantItHarder: **Single-matriarch run:** reach 100 with only one mother, leaning entirely on multiples.
Forbid maids and all hired help — the matriarch does every chore as well as every nappy.
milestones: Survive your first batch of toddlers
Pass the title to a second matriarch (or push past where most need to)
variations: The "**no-help hard mode**" (no maids at all), or a themed version where every child is named alphabetically or by birth order. ---
scoring: Your score is about **efficiency**: the fewer matriarchs (generations) you use to reach 100, the better. A single-matriarch run is near-impossible; two or three is an excellent result. The earlier "Cheesecake" ancestor of this challenge aimed for 100 in 50 births or fewer.
credit: A community classic descended from the Sims 2 "Cheesecake Challenge"; the modern, regularly-updated official ruleset is maintained by **Snarky Sims (Snarky Sims Witch)**.
allowed: Find each father anywhere in the world; use Try for Baby or risky woohoo.
Use MCCC (optional) to manage the townie dating pool.
Move grown children out at young adult to make room, and promote the eldest daughter to matriarch when the current one can no longer give birth.
notAllowed: Never use the same father twice.
No nannies or babysitters — the matriarch and older children raise every baby themselves.
The matriarch may not hold a rabbit-hole job that pulls her from the kids (work-from-home only), and no money cheats in the purist version.
objectives: Birth your first child to a unique father
Raise the first batch through the toddler stage
Hand off to a second matriarch (the eldest daughter)
baseGameVersion: Fully base-game. MCCC is an optional convenience mod for managing fathers, not a requirement.

title: Not So Berry
backstory: A rainbow legacy for people who love berry Sims but can't stand actual berry Sims. Ten generations, each built around a single colour and a wildly specific personality — a mischievous mint scientist, a heartless rose politician, a clumsy yellow astronaut — each one forcing you into corners of the game you'd normally never touch.
bestFor: Players who want a legacy with built-in variety and story
premise: A rainbow legacy for people who love berry Sims but can't stand actual berry Sims.
goal: Complete **ten generations**, each heir fulfilling their colour's assigned **career, aspiration, traits and special rules**.

setup.label: Lot
setup.value: Your choice (some generations specify their own living rules)

setup.label: Funds
setup.value: Default; money cheats used sparingly at most

generalRules: Play in normal live mode at normal lifespan — no speeding through.
Each heir expresses their generation's colour (hair / makeup / clothing); spouses are exempt and can look however you like.
Each generation must complete **both** its assigned career and aspiration unless a generation's rules say otherwise.
One heir carries the bloodline to the next colour.
specificRules: Gen 1 — Mint: Scientist / Chief of Mischief. Traits Vegetarian, Jealous, Materialistic. Master Mischief & Logic; complete the Elements collection. A luxury-loving prankster scientist.
Gen 2 — Rose: Politician / Serial Romantic. Traits Snob, Hot-Headed, Romantic. Master Charisma. Only **one** child; leave someone at the altar; don't marry until elder.

Gen 3 — Yellow: Astronaut / Nerd Brain. Traits Clumsy, Ambitious, Loner. Master Rocket Science & Handiness; build a rocket and visit Sixam; find the secret lot in Oasis Springs. No close ties except the Gen-1 grandparent until that grandparent dies.
Gen 4 — Grey: Athlete / Bodybuilder. Traits Active, Slob, Music Lover. Master Singing, Parenting & Athletic. Endure three failed relationships before settling.
Gen 5 — Plum: Entertainer (via Fast Food → Doctor → Entertainer) / Renaissance Sim. Traits Genius, Noncommittal, Dance Machine. An indecisive oddball: works as a doctor, then quits to chase dancing.

Gen 6 — Orange: Criminal / Public Enemy. Traits Evil, Self-Assured, Glutton. Master Baking & Charisma. Live in an apartment with the **Needs TLC** trait the whole young-adult life; have **twins** as the only children (cheating for twins allowed); keep insisting you're a criminal mastermind that nobody believes.
Gen 7 — Pink: Business → Writer / Best Selling Author. Grew up poor; works the family business job, then finally quits to write romance novels. Hopeless romantic, hopelessly unflirty.
Gen 8 — Peach: Detective + part-time Comedian / (comedy/detective persona). Splits life between solving cases and chasing laughs.
Gen 9 — Green: Tech Guru / Computer Whiz. The cheerful family geek; master Programming on the way up.

Gen 10 — Blue: Critic / Super Parent. Traits Gloomy, Perfectionist, Family-Oriented. Master Photography, Cooking & Parenting; marry a high-school sweetheart and stay together to the end; adopt at least one child; hide a single affair. The bloodline ends here.
strategyTips: Write each heir's colour, career, aspiration and rules into an in-game note so you never lose track mid-generation.
Lock your ten colour palettes early (which hair/clothing swatches "read" as each colour) so Mint and Green don't blur together by the late generations.
Time your births: only have the next heir once the current one is well into their career, so you have time to finish their goals.
wantItHarder: Complete every aspiration **without** using satisfaction-point reward traits to shortcut it.
Run it as true berry Sims — colour the skin too — and add the Legacy Challenge's single-lot rule on top.
milestones: Finish Mint's brief (Scientist + Chief of Mischief)
variations: Many extended "Not So Berry 2.0 / all-packs" versions add generations 11+; you can also remap any pack-locked career to one you own (the challenge openly supports skipping or swapping generations for packs you don't have). ---
credit: Created by **lilsimsie and alwaysimming** in 2017. The colour ladder above follows their original brief; community "extended" versions add later generations.

generationLadder.notes: Traits Vegetarian, Jealous, Materialistic. Master Mischief & Logic; complete the Elements collection. A luxury-loving prankster scientist.
Traits Snob, Hot-Headed, Romantic. Master Charisma. Only **one** child; leave someone at the altar; don't marry until elder.

Traits Clumsy, Ambitious, Loner. Master Rocket Science & Handiness; build a rocket and visit Sixam; find the secret lot in Oasis Springs. No close ties except the Gen-1 grandparent until that grandparent dies.
Traits Active, Slob, Music Lover. Master Singing, Parenting & Athletic. Endure three failed relationships before settling.
Traits Genius, Noncommittal, Dance Machine. An indecisive oddball: works as a doctor, then quits to chase dancing.

Traits Evil, Self-Assured, Glutton. Master Baking & Charisma. Live in an apartment with the **Needs TLC** trait the whole young-adult life; have **twins** as the only children (cheating for twins allowed); keep insisting you're a criminal mastermind that nobody believes.
Grew up poor; works the family business job, then finally quits to write romance novels. Hopeless romantic, hopelessly unflirty.
Splits life between solving cases and chasing laughs.
The cheerful family geek; master Programming on the way up.

Traits Gloomy, Perfectionist, Family-Oriented. Master Photography, Cooking & Parenting; marry a high-school sweetheart and stay together to the end; adopt at least one child; hide a single affair. The bloodline ends here.

allowed: Adjust or swap any rule to fit the packs you own — the challenge is explicitly flexible.
Theme each heir's hair, clothes and home décor to their colour.
Use money cheats if you like — it's a relaxed, story-first challenge.
notAllowed: Don't change a generation's assigned career, aspiration or traits — that's the whole point.
Don't skip a generation's special rules.
No ageing heirs up before they've met their required milestones.
objectives: Complete Gen 1 (Mint): Scientist + Chief of Mischief
baseGameVersion: It leans on several packs, but lilsimsie publishes swaps; wherever you lack a pack, substitute a fitting base-game career or aspiration for that generation.

title: Random Legacy
backstory: A legacy where you're not in charge — the dice are. Every generation, a roll decides your heir's career, their traits, how many children they're allowed, and what curveball rule they have to live under. You bring the parenting; fate brings the chaos.
bestFor: Legacy fans who want surprise and replayability
premise: A legacy where you're not in charge — the dice are.
goal: Complete **ten generations**, each played strictly to its randomly rolled rules.
gettingStarted: Create one founder, move onto a starter lot, set funds to §0, and roll the founder's generation before you play them.
Roll each generation's **career, trait(s), partner type, child count** and any special rule (use a dice app or a Sims random-legacy generator).
generalRules: No money cheats after the §0 start.
One heir continues each generation.
Roll *before* you play each heir, and play them exactly as rolled.
specificRules: The roll is binding — no re-rolling a result you dislike.
The rolled child-count caps how many children that generation may have.
Any rolled "special rule" (e.g. must marry a stranger, can't hold a job, must have twins) genuinely applies.
strategyTips: Keep a written generation log of every roll so the rules are unambiguous later.
Build a flexible house — a rolled child-count of six needs very different space from a roll of one.
Lean into bad rolls as story beats rather than fighting them; the awkwardness is the fun.
wantItHarder: Add a rolled **misfortune** each generation (a death, a fire, a job loss) that must actually happen.
Roll the heir's *aspiration* too, and require it completed.
variations: Use a published random-legacy roll table for structured categories, or make your own themed roll deck. ---
credit: A long-standing community format (the Random Legacy Challenge) with many roll-table variants.
allowed: Use any randomiser — dice, an app, or the in-game randomise — to roll each generation's rules.
Re-roll only when a result needs a pack you don't own.
Use `bb.moveobjects`.
notAllowed: No cherry-picking, and no re-rolling a result you simply dislike.
No money cheats.
Each generation must be played strictly to its rolled rules.
objectives: Roll Gen 1's rules (children, relationship, goal, income, misc)
Complete Gen 1 to its rolled rules
Honour a roll you really didn't want
baseGameVersion: Fully base-game — just re-roll any result that would require DLC you don't have.

title: The Crowned Dynasty
backstory: A crown is easy to win and brutally hard to keep. Rise from minor noble to the highest royal rank, then hold the throne for three generations while rivals circle, scandals brew, and every heir has to earn their standing all over again.
bestFor: Roleplayers who want pageantry and intrigue
premise: A crown is easy to win and brutally hard to keep.
goal: Rise to the **highest royal rank**, then hold the crown across **three generations**.
gettingStarted: Create one aspiring noble and move into an estate befitting their ambitions. Modest royal seed funds.
generalRules: Build standing only through legitimate noble duties, decrees and courtly life.
One heir carries the crown to the next generation.
No money cheats after setup.
specificRules: Each heir must **earn** their rank — no inheriting the top tier outright.
Reputation matters: scandals carry real consequences you must roleplay through.
The bloodline must stay crowned for three full generations; falling to the lowest tier ends the reign.
strategyTips: Treat reputation as your most precious resource — a single scandal can undo a generation of work.
Marry strategically; alliances strengthen the dynasty's standing.
Start grooming each heir's standing early, so the crown passes without a power vacuum.
wantItHarder: Create a **rival house** you refuse to ally with — every marriage and decree must work against their claim.
Hold the crown for five generations instead of three.
variations: Pair with Not So Berry colours for a "royal rainbow," or with the Legacy single-lot rule for a palace that never moves. ---
credit: Original — Plumbob, designed around the Royalty & Legacy expansion.
allowed: Build standing through legitimate royal duties and decrees.
Arrange marriages and alliances; use `bb.moveobjects`.
notAllowed: No rank or standing cheats.
No abandoning the throne mid-generation; one heir carries the crown.
No money cheats.
baseGameVersion: Requires Royalty & Legacy for the rank and court system — there's no true base-game version. The closest stand-in is a roleplay monarchy with no mechanics behind it.

title: Single Parent Scramble
backstory: One adult. Five kids. No co-parent walking through that door, ever. It's the daily juggle every solo parent knows — homework, tantrums, dinner and a job — turned into a full challenge, and there's no second pair of hands coming to save you.
bestFor: Players who love hands-on family chaos
premise: One adult.
goal: Raise **five children** to happy, well-adjusted young adults as a single parent, with **no co-parent** ever in the home.
gettingStarted: Create one parent (expecting or about to adopt). Move into a modest family home.
generalRules: Only one adult in the household at any time.
No money cheats after setup.
Children must reach young adulthood with strong character values.
specificRules: No partner ever moves in — dates may happen, but they never stay.
The parent does all the parenting; no live-in help.
All five children must launch into young adulthood healthy and happy.
strategyTips: Stagger births — five toddlers at once is unwinnable; space them so older kids can semi-self-manage while you handle the youngest.
Parenthood's character values are your scoreboard; consistent discipline and encouragement pay off at every life stage.
A work-from-home or flexible income keeps you present for the needs crunches.
wantItHarder: Hold down a **full-time career** the entire time, with no nanny or maid ever hired.
Raise the count from five children to seven.
milestones: First child to the child stage
variations: The *adopted-only* version (every child adopted), or a single-parent-on-§0 rags-to-riches crossover. ---
credit: Original — Plumbob.
allowed: Hold any job and use school and daycare as normal.
Use `bb.moveobjects`; ask friends/family to visit and help occasionally.
notAllowed: No co-parent, partner or spouse living in the home — ever.
No live-in nanny.
No money cheats.
objectives: Juggle work and a toddler entirely solo
Keep every child's needs and school grades green
See all five become happy, well-adjusted young adults
baseGameVersion: Fully base-game. Parenthood adds character values to measure 'well-adjusted'; base-game uses school grades plus green needs instead.

title: The Orphanage
backstory: A big house, a rotating cast of children who need a home, and one guardian holding it all together. As one child grows up and moves on to their own life, another arrives at the door — the household never empties, and the heart of it never closes.
bestFor: Players who love nonstop child-raising
premise: A big house, a rotating cast of children who need a home, and one guardian holding it all together.
goal: Raise **twelve adopted children** to young adulthood, never letting the home hold fewer than four kids at once (until the final wind-down).
gettingStarted: Create one guardian, move into a large multi-bedroom home, and adopt your first cohort.
generalRules: Children are **adopted**, never born into the household.
No money cheats after setup.
Keep at least four children in care at all times until you're finishing the run.
specificRules: As one child ages out and moves on, adopt another to replace them.
The guardian may work, but no co-parent moves in.
Twelve children total must reach young adulthood from your care.
strategyTips: Adopt across life stages, not all as toddlers — a mix of ages keeps the workload survivable.
Older kids and teens can help with chores and homework, easing the crunch.
Budget for the constant churn of beds, food and school supplies; a stable income is essential.
wantItHarder: Run it on a single modest income with no money cheats and no more than two hired helpers ever.
Keep at least **six** children in care at all times.
variations: A themed "boarding house" for teens only, or a multigenerational version where grown orphans return to help run the home. ---
credit: Original — Plumbob.
allowed: Adopt repeatedly and fill the household to the 8-Sim cap.
Let older children help raise the younger ones; earn from any source.
Use `bb.moveobjects`.
notAllowed: Never fewer than four children at once, until the final wind-down.
No biological children — every child is adopted.
No money cheats.
objectives: Keep four or more kids in the home at all times
Raise the first child to young adult and move them out
Cycle in new adoptees to stay above four
Raise twelve adoptees to young adulthood in total
baseGameVersion: Fully base-game.

title: Black Sheep Legacy
backstory: Every child rebels against their parents — but in this dynasty it's the law. Each heir must become the deliberate opposite of the one before: where a parent was a tidy office-working homebody, the child is a messy artist who never sits still. Ten generations of family members who can't stand each other's choices.
bestFor: Players who want built-in variety each generation
premise: Every child rebels against their parents — but in this dynasty it's the law.
goal: Complete **ten generations** where every heir contradicts their parent in every major way.
gettingStarted: Create one founder however you like — they set the baseline that Generation 2 must rebel against.
generalRules: One heir continues each generation.
No money cheats after setup.
Each heir is raised in, then rejects, the parent's way of life.
specificRules: Each heir's **career** must differ from their parent's.
Heir **traits** must contrast the parent's wherever possible (neat↔slob, social↔loner, and so on).
The heir's whole lifestyle flips each generation.
strategyTips: Note each parent's traits and career before designing the heir, so the contrast is genuine and not accidental overlap.
Use teen years to seed the rebellion — let the heir visibly diverge before they age up.
Keep a generation log so by Gen 8 you still remember what's already been "used."
wantItHarder: Heirs must also reject the parent's home aesthetic — fully redecorate the lot every generation.
Contrast must extend to aspiration *and* preferred world/lot type.
variations: A two-line "feud" version where two black-sheep branches compete down the generations. ---
credit: Original — Plumbob.
allowed: Use any traits, careers and aspirations to build each heir's opposite.
Use `bb.moveobjects`.
notAllowed: Each heir must oppose their parent in trait, career and aspiration — no overlap at all.
The heir must be born in-game.
No money cheats.
objectives: Found Gen 1 with defined traits, career and aspiration
Raise a Gen 2 heir who contradicts them in every major way
Document each generation's set of opposites
baseGameVersion: Fully base-game.

title: Generational Curse
backstory: Something is wrong with this family, and it has been for generations. A misfortune — early deaths, ruined romances, fortunes that crumble — strikes every heir, growing stronger with each one, until at last a descendant finds the way to break it. Or doesn't.
bestFor: Story players who love a dark throughline
premise: Something is wrong with this family, and it has been for generations.
goal: Carry the bloodline far enough that one heir finally **breaks** the chosen family curse.
gettingStarted: Create a founder and define the curse before you play: pick one recurring misfortune and the exact, earned condition that can break it.
Decide your curse (e.g. "no heir lives past adult until the curse breaks") and its break-condition.
generalRules: One heir continues each generation.
The curse genuinely befalls each heir — no quietly skipping it.
No money cheats after setup.
specificRules: Define one curse at the start (early deaths, doomed romances, lost fortunes, etc.).
The curse can only be lifted by a single defined, earned condition.
No heir may simply cure themselves outside that condition.
strategyTips: Make the break-condition demanding but achievable (e.g. "an heir must max a specific skill *and* marry for love") so the payoff feels earned.
Roleplay the dread — let the family fear the curse, name it, try and fail to outrun it.
Keep heirs producing successors early, since the curse may strike before you expect.
wantItHarder: The curse **escalates** each generation, getting harder to survive until it's broken.
Add a "false hope" rule: one earlier heir must come agonisingly close and fail.
variations: A literal occult curse (a haunting, a vampire bloodline) using an occult pack, or a "lucky vs cursed" twin-line version. ---
credit: Original — Plumbob.
allowed: Choose a family 'curse' (e.g., every heir dies young, never finds love, or stays poor) and a clear break-condition.
Use `bb.moveobjects`.
notAllowed: No heir may break the curse until your stated break-condition is met.
The curse rules bind every generation until broken.
No money cheats.
objectives: Define the curse and its break-condition
Live Gen 1 fully under the curse
Reach the generation that meets the break-condition
Have an heir finally break the curse
baseGameVersion: Fully base-game.

title: The Wonder Child
backstory: One child. Every childhood skill maxed, the aspiration done, perfect character values — all before the teen birthday. It's a sprint against the clock of childhood itself, and it asks whether you can pour everything into raising one genuinely exceptional kid.
bestFor: Players who love optimised, focused parenting
premise: One child.
goal: Take one child to **maxed childhood skills, a completed aspiration and top character values** before they age into a teen.
gettingStarted: Create two parents and one focus child (or have the child in-game). A comfortable home with skill-building objects helps.
generalRules: No aging cheats to buy extra childhood time.
No skill cheats — everything is earned.
The clock is the child's natural progression to teen.
specificRules: Max all four childhood skills (Creativity, Mental, Motor, Social) before the teen birthday.
Complete the child's aspiration.
Reach top marks in all Parenthood character values.
strategyTips: Keep the child's needs topped up constantly — every wasted hour is one you can't get back before they age up.
Rotate skill activities to avoid boredom debuffs slowing progress.
Both parents actively coaching (homework help, encouragement) accelerates character values.
wantItHarder: Achieve it all as a **single parent** holding a full-time job.
Add a sibling who must *not* be neglected while you focus on the wonder child.
variations: Extend it into a "wonder family" where every child of the generation must hit the same bar. ---
credit: Based on the community **Wonder Child Challenge**.
allowed: Let parents and family teach skills using toys, books and activities.
Use Parenthood's character-value lessons.
Use `bb.moveobjects`.
notAllowed: No ageing the child up early.
No skill cheats.
Every target must be hit before the teen birthday.
objectives: Reach the top of all character values (Parenthood)
Finish everything before the teen birthday
baseGameVersion: Character values need Parenthood; the base-game version instead maxes every child skill, completes a child aspiration, and earns straight-A grades before the teen birthday.

title: Townie Takeover
backstory: Your bloodline doesn't just grow — it absorbs. Generation by generation, you marry into and inherit every major pre-made family in the world, until every famous surname in town is just another branch of your enormous family tree.

title: PlantSim Garden

bestFor: Gardeners who want a gentle occult twist

Stay in PlantSim form as long as you can (it's temporary — chain it by growing more Forbidden Fruit).

title: The Apocalypse Challenge
backstory: Twenty years ago the reactor melted down, and your corner of the world has been clawing back from the ashes ever since. Life is buried under a mountain of restrictions — and the only way to lift them is to send your family, one Sim and one career at a time, to the very top of their profession, slowly rebuilding civilisation skill by hard-won skill.
bestFor: Players who want the genre's hardest, longest puzzle
premise: Twenty years ago the reactor melted down, and your corner of the world has been clawing back from the ashes ever since.
goal: **Lift every apocalypse restriction.** Each restriction is tied to a career; you lift it by getting a Sim to the **top (level 10) of that career**. The run ends when all restrictions are gone.
gettingStarted: Create or import a single founder (any age, even pre-skilled — unlike the Legacy Challenge). Move them onto an empty lot and quit any job; their past doesn't count.
Quit any career; begin under the full restriction list. (Tip: start the save on a Sunday for the "swap-meet" buy restriction.)

setup.label: Lot
setup.value: Empty lot (you may never move during the run)

generalRules: You start under **all** restrictions at once; life is brutal by design.
A restriction is lifted only when a Sim maxes the career it's tied to — then that freedom applies to the whole household.
**Each Sim may lift only one restriction in their lifetime** — so this is a generational marathon; you need heirs to lift them all.
Only bloodline Sims and the spouses of heirs may lift restrictions; the family may never move lots.
specificRules: **Culinary → Chef:** no ovens or appliances except the cheapest grill, until a Sim maxes Culinary.
**Tech Guru:** only the Tech Guru Sim may use a computer, and only the cheapest one, until maxed.
**Retail (teen):** you may only buy or replace objects on **Sundays** (the weekly swap meet).
**Athlete:** no smoke detectors or sprinklers; non-athletic children, teens and elders can't even leave the lot.
Many careers cross-gate each other (some can't be taken until another is unlocked first).
strategyTips: Lift the restriction that annoys *you* most first — usually Culinary (to cook) — but note some careers need a pre-skilled founder to even start (e.g. Chef needs gourmet cooking the grill can't build).
Plan your bloodline: with one restriction per Sim, you need a steady supply of heirs, so don't neglect having children.
Map the unlock order in advance — career cross-gating means a wrong early pick can stall you for a generation.
wantItHarder: Add the Eco Lifestyle **off-grid** layer: stay off the grid until a Sim maxes the Civic Planner career.
Forbid pre-skilled founders — start from a blank Sim.
variations: Use the base-game-only ruleset for a defined finish line, or the all-packs version for an open-ended epic. ---
scoring: **Fewer in-game days to lift all restrictions = a better score.** The original base-game target is roughly 25 careers; with every pack it can exceed 50.
credit: Created by **Pinstar** (originally for The Sims 2), adapted to The Sims 4 and maintained/updated by **poopsiesims**, with a popular modified version by **Raerei**.
allowed: Survive off wild/foraged plants and fishing from day one; cook grilled fish on the cheapest grill.
Use the MoveObjects cheat — but only after you've lifted the career restriction that gates building.
Buy only the cheapest version of any object you're allowed to purchase; move in one spouse per generation (without keeping their funds).
notAllowed: No cheats beyond setup and the gated MoveObjects, and no advantage-giving mods/CC.
No ageing Sims up early, and no moving to a different lot — ever.

A Sim may lift only ONE career's restrictions (the first career they top); until lifted: no TV/internet/radio, computer only for job tasks, phone only to get/quit a job, shopping only on Sundays, one stack of food until Athlete is lifted, and building confined to a small footprint.
objectives: Found a jobless Sim on an empty lot (all restrictions active)
Lift the Culinary restriction → real cooking returns
Lift the Athlete restriction → carry more than one stack
Lift the Tech Guru/Engineer restriction → unlock building & MoveObjects
Lift the Civic Planner/utilities restriction → power and water return
Lift every remaining career restriction (one Sim each) — challenge complete
baseGameVersion: Pinstar's original is base-game only — about 25 careers, fully playable with no DLC. Owning more packs simply ADDS careers and restrictions, making the run longer, never blocking it.

title: Black Widow
backstory: Charming, beautiful, and absolutely lethal to anyone who says "I do." Your Sim marries for money, mourns for a moment, and moves straight on to the next wealthy suitor — leaving a tidy little graveyard of well-funded ex-spouses behind the manor.
bestFor: Players who want the genre's classic storytelling villain run
premise: Charming, beautiful, and absolutely lethal to anyone who says "I do." Your Sim marries for money, mourns for a moment, and moves straight on to the next wealthy suitor — leaving a tidy little graveyard of well-funded ex-spouses behind the manor.
goal: Marry and outlive **ten spouses**, inheriting each fortune, never working a day.
gettingStarted: Create a young-adult Black Widow with the traits **Snob, Romantic, Materialistic** (many versions add **Jealous**) and the **Serial Romantic** aspiration. Set lifespan to normal and turn **aging off for every Sim except the Black Widow**. Move into a cheap starter home.
Create the Black Widow; set aging off for all other Sims in game options.

setup.label: Lot
setup.value: A cheap starter home (it must grow over the run)

setup.label: Aging
setup.value: On for the Black Widow, off for everyone else

generalRules: **No job, ever** — all money comes from spouses. You may earn from selling collectibles, fishing or woodworking *only* before your first marriage or between spouses.
**No money cheats.**
**No repairs** — when something breaks, you must *replace* it with Simoleons (a widow doesn't do hard labour).
specificRules: **Only marry single Sims.** To marry an already-partnered Sim, delete their other household members first (so their funds transfer to you).
After each marriage, host a **Dinner Party** and earn at least a **silver** rating — proof you're the "perfect spouse."
Take a new love interest and **get caught cheating** by your spouse (it's the motive). You may juggle many love interests at once (and it speeds Serial Romantic).
Then end the marriage — the Black Widow must **witness** each spouse's death — and keep the urn/tombstone, labelled **"Killed by the Black Widow."**
The home must **grow in size and value** with each new spouse. Letting a spouse die of natural causes is a failure of the role (and costs satisfaction).
Outlive **ten** spouses.
strategyTips: Build **Cooking** and **Charisma** early — they're what get your dinner parties to silver and your suitors to the altar.
Target wealthy or employed Sims; a spouse who already has a job keeps earning for you after they move in.
Keep a graveyard out back with labelled tombstones — it's both the scoreboard and the storytelling centrepiece.
wantItHarder: **Legendary mode:** finish all ten spouses *and* the Serial Romantic aspiration before your Black Widow ages out of young adult.
At least three spouses must themselves have the Jealous trait.
variations: A vampire Black Widow (immortal, aging never an issue), or a "merry widower" male-led version. ---
scoring: The classic ruleset has difficulty tiers. Bonus points include **+2000** for completing the Serial Romantic aspiration, **+3000** for finishing all ten spouses **before becoming an Elder**, and a further **+3000** for the top "Specialist" difficulty. Some versions also require completing the **Mansion Baron** aspiration.
credit: Created by **Simalot (aka lotlot80)**, with roots in The Sims 2; widely adapted across the community.
allowed: Marry single Sims and host a silver-rated (or better) dinner party for each marriage.
Let the home grow in value with every spouse; keep aging on for the widow, off for the spouses.
Use `bb.moveobjects`.
notAllowed: No job and no money cheats — every Simoleon comes from spouses' inheritances.
No repairing broken objects — replace them with new ones, draining the inherited fortune.
Only ever marry single Sims.
objectives: Grow the home's value with each marriage
baseGameVersion: Fully base-game. The original adds Legendary scoring (all ten spouses before the widow becomes an Elder) and Specialist bonuses for trickier spouse traits.

Children are played exactly as their genetics produce them.

notAllowed: No cosmetic edits to the founders or children after randomising.

title: The Decades Challenge
backstory: Begin in gaslit 1890, and march your bloodline forward one decade at a time — through wars, women's suffrage, the arrival of electricity and the telephone, all the way to the present day. Each decade rewrites the rules your family must live by. It's part legacy, part history lesson, and one of the most beloved sagas in the whole game.
bestFor: Players who love history and the longest of long games
premise: Begin in gaslit 1890, and march your bloodline forward one decade at a time — through wars, women's suffrage, the arrival of electricity and the telephone, all the way to the present day.
goal: Live from the **1890s to the present day**, one decade per generation, following era-accurate rules for each.
gettingStarted: Create one young-adult founder or a married young-adult couple (you may add a parent for an authentic multi-generational 1890s home). Use only your starting funds.
Money cheats are allowed **only as a repayable loan** — pay it back, plus 10% interest, before your founders die.

setup.label: Aging
setup.value: On, normal until the 1950s, then long

setup.label: Sims
setup.value: One founder or a married couple

generalRules: Each decade has its own rules on careers, aspirations, clothing, objects and technology — research and honour them.
No cheats except the repayable starter loan.
Advance one decade per generation.
specificRules: **Technology gating:** no electricity in the 1890s (candles and lanterns); cell phones and computers are heavily restricted until the decades they'd actually exist (a computer may stand in for a "typewriter" for writing only).

**The wars:** for WWI (1910s), WWII, Korea and Vietnam, eligible Sims are "sent to war" — simulated by feeding them a slice of **Cowplant** cake and rolling a die (odds = they die, evens = they return, traumatised, gaining a negative trait). Grow your Cowplant before 1910.
**Era-specific life rules** (inheritance, who may work, who may marry) change each decade to reflect the period — adapt them to your comfort.
strategyTips: Grow a healthy Cowplant *well* before 1910 — the war mechanic depends on it, and a hungry household can lose it.
Tackle one decade's custom content and rules at a time rather than prepping all fourteen at once.
Use the **likes/dislikes** system to stop Sims autonomously using anachronistic objects (set a 1920s Sim to dislike gaming, for instance).
wantItHarder: Play one of the **extended** versions that begin in the 1700s (or even the 1300s) with extra historical death-rolls.
Track exact in-game years and age Sims up precisely to the calendar.
variations: Localise it to your own country's history, or run a "soft" aesthetic-only version that keeps the era looks but drops the harsher rules. ---

title: The Asylum Challenge
backstory: You control exactly one Sim in a house of eight — and the other seven do whatever they please, all day, every day. Your job is to keep your head (and theirs) and complete your goals amid total free-willed chaos, on a shoestring budget, in a deliberately rickety home. It is the iconic hands-off challenge, and it is gloriously hard.

setup.label: Lot
setup.value: A residential lot; at least one **negative** lot trait

generalRules: You control only your main Sim — never click or direct the other seven; their high free will runs everything they do.
Furnish with the **cheapest** objects only; no items that boost emotions (unless earned as career rewards) and nothing that prevents fires.
Limited beds, seating and skill objects (tighter on harder modes).
specificRules: Your main Sim completes 2 / 3 / 4 aspirations (by difficulty) to "earn their freedom." Certain long or household-dependent aspirations are banned (Successful Lineage, Big Happy Family, Super Parent, Fabulously Wealthy, Mansion Baron, City Native).
Income comes from selling your main Sim's creations (paintings, books, collectibles); the uncontrollable Sims may only work the low-paying Criminal career, and you can't manage their progress.
A **curfew** limits leaving the lot, and you must travel with all eight; when a housemate dies, you remove seats/beds per the mode.
strategyTips: Put a money-making skill object (the easel is the classic) front and centre — it's your only reliable income while you babysit chaos.
Fire safety matters: cheap stoves plus no fire-prevention allowed means cooking is genuinely dangerous — build cooking skill fast or lean on no-cook food.
Make an **Asylum club** (Get Together) of just the eight so you can at least nudge group behaviour through club activities and perks.
wantItHarder: Play **hard mode**: four aspirations, all-negative lot traits, no hired help, the tightest furniture limits.
Forbid the easel-and-sell economy and survive on gardening alone.
milestones: No deaths through the halfway mark
variations: The "compassionate" group-home reskin, or a "Big Brother" cousin where you narrate weekly elimination drama instead of chasing aspirations. ---
scoring: A classic optional system rewards **speed and survival**: points for skills, friends and aspiration points; minus points for every day spent inside and a heavy penalty for each housemate who dies. Fewest days, everyone alive = the best score.
credit: A community classic that dates back to The Sims 2; the original Sims 4 author is uncredited, but the widely-used ruleset (easy/medium/hard) circulates on the EA forums.
allowed: Use money cheats for the initial build only; turn free will to maximum.
Make an 'Asylum' Club of the eight (Get Together) to nudge group behaviour; earn money from the controllable Sim's sold creations.
notAllowed: Control only your main Sim — never click or direct the other seven.
Cheapest furniture only; no emotion-boosting objects (unless career rewards) and nothing that prevents fires.
Banned long/household aspirations (Successful Lineage, Big Happy Family, Super Parent, Fabulously Wealthy, Mansion Baron, City Native); a curfew limits leaving, and you travel only with all eight.
objectives: Create eight Erratic Sims and a sparse home
Reach the halfway mark with no housemate deaths
baseGameVersion: Fully base-game. Many players run a kinder reskin (a chaotic shared house or group home) — the mechanic underneath is simply controlling one free-willed Sim among eight.

bestFor: Players who want a tense coming-of-age story
premise: No parents, no permission, no safety net.
goal: Survive alone as a **teenager** — supporting yourself and dodging "the authorities" — until you age up to young adult.
gettingStarted: Move a lone teen into a bare lot (or play them homeless), with no adults in the household and almost no money.

setup.label: Lot
setup.value: A bare lot or rough squat

generalRules: No adult lives in or supports the household.
Income comes only from teen-appropriate means (part-time job, odd jobs, selling finds).
Survive to the young-adult birthday.
specificRules: The teen keeps their grades up enough to avoid "trouble" (roleplay the threat of being sent back).
No adult help, money or move-ins.
Stay fed, sheltered and out of crisis until aging up.
strategyTips: A part-time job plus selling forageables covers the basics — line up income on day one.
Keep school performance from cratering; roleplay a falling grade as a near-miss with the authorities.
Use public venues (gyms, libraries) for free hygiene, fun and warmth to stretch your few Simoleons.
wantItHarder: Start fully **homeless** with no lot at all, living rough until you can rent a room.

Use `bb.moveobjects`.
notAllowed: No adult lives in or financially supports the household.

title: Desert Island Story
