Start from scratch; **no cheats** toward the milestone.
generalRules: The milestone must be reached within seven days.
No cheats toward the goal.
Day eight is too late.
specificRules: Define the milestone precisely before you begin.
Balance needs and progress across the week.
Hit the target by the end of day seven.
strategyTips: Plan the week backwards from the milestone — know roughly what each day must deliver.
Keep mood high; seven days leaves no room for sad, slow progress.
Pick a milestone that's ambitious-but-possible in a week, then push your method to its limit.
wantItHarder: Shrink it to **three** days.
Stack two milestones into the same week.
variations: A recurring "weekly wonder" series, a new milestone every in-game week. ---
credit: Original — Plumbob, a seven-day sprint format.
allowed: Use any non-cheat means toward a set seven-day milestone; use `bb.moveobjects`.
notAllowed: Hit the milestone within seven in-game days.

objectives: Set the milestone and start day one
Make progress through days two and three
Reach the milestone by day seven

title: The Coin-Flip Life
backstory: Marry them or dump them? Take the job or quit? Buy the house or blow the savings? Your Sim doesn't decide — a coin does. Surrender every big choice to pure chance and follow wherever the flips lead.
bestFor: Players who want to give up the wheel
premise: Marry them or dump them? Take the job or quit? Buy the house or blow the savings? Your Sim doesn't decide — a coin does.
goal: Play a Sim's life where **every major decision is settled by a real coin or dice roll**.
gettingStarted: Create a Sim and commit the rule: at every fork in the road, flip a coin (or roll) and obey it.

setup.label: Sims
setup.value: One Sim at the mercy of chance

generalRules: Every major life decision is decided by a real flip or roll.
The result is binding — no overruling fate.
Follow the consequences wherever they lead.
specificRules: Define which decisions count (career, romance, moves, big purchases).
Flip/roll *before* acting, and act accordingly.
No re-flipping a result you dislike.
strategyTips: Set clear "heads/tails" meanings before each flip so there's no fudging after the fact.
Use dice for multi-option choices (1–2 = job A, 3–4 = job B, etc.).
Lean into bad flips as story — the chaos is the entire appeal.
wantItHarder: Flip for *minor* decisions too, not just the big ones.
A single "ruin" result on any flip must be honoured fully, however costly.
milestones: A whole life shaped by chance
variations: A whole household run entirely by the coin, each member at fate's mercy. ---
credit: Original — Plumbob, a chance-driven format.
allowed: Use a real coin or dice for every major decision and abide by the result; use `bb.moveobjects`.
notAllowed: No overriding a roll — every major choice is decided by chance.
No cheats.
objectives: Honour a roll you didn't want
Play a full life entirely by chance

title: Last Sim Standing
backstory: Eight Sims, ten days, one question: who's still thriving at the end? Set a full house loose, barely intervene, and watch which Sims flourish and which fall apart. A low-effort survival contest you mostly just narrate.
bestFor: Players who like hands-off, watch-it-unfold play
premise: Eight Sims, ten days, one question: who's still thriving at the end? Set a full house loose, barely intervene, and watch which Sims flourish and which fall apart.
goal: Start a **full house** and see which Sims are still **thriving after ten days** of mostly hands-off play.
gettingStarted: Fill a household with eight varied Sims, set high free will, and intervene as little as possible.
generalRules: Minimal player intervention — let Sims run on free will.
Track each Sim's wellbeing over ten days.
The "winner" is whoever's thriving most at day ten.
specificRules: Define "thriving" up front (mood, friendships, skills, money).
Don't micromanage — nudge only when a rule allows.
Score everyone at the end of day ten.
strategyTips: Cast a deliberately mixed bag — some self-sufficient, some chaotic — for an interesting spread by day ten.
Decide your intervention rule in advance (e.g. "only act to prevent death") and stick to it.
Keep notes each day so the final "standings" are clear.
wantItHarder: Zero intervention at all — pure observation for ten days.
Extend to a full thirty-day "season."
variations: A "weekly eviction" version where the lowest-scoring Sim leaves each few days. ---
credit: Original — Plumbob, a hands-off survival-contest format.

notAllowed: Minimal intervention — set and keep your hands-off rule, no micromanaging.
No cheats.
objectives: Fill the house with eight Sims
Assess the household at day ten
baseGameVersion: Fully base-game.

title: The Lottery Life
backstory: You don't pick anything. A random generator does — your Sim's traits, their job, where they live, who they marry. Each round hands you a fresh hand of cards, and your only job is to play whatever luck deals as well as you can.
bestFor: Players who love surrendering to randomness
premise: You don't pick anything.
goal: Play only with whatever a **random generator hands you each round**, adapting to your luck.
gettingStarted: Use a randomiser for every setup choice — traits, aspiration, career, world, partner — and play the hand you're dealt.
Randomise each setup element; **no re-rolls**.
generalRules: Every choice that *can* be randomised, is.
No re-rolling results you don't like.
Adapt your play to whatever you're given.
specificRules: Randomise traits, aspiration, career and home before playing.
Honour each result fully.
Re-roll a fresh "life" each round when you want a new hand.
strategyTips: Decide your randomisation method up front (an app, dice, CAS randomise) for consistency.
Make the best of clashing results — a shy Sim in a fame career is a story, not a mistake.
Keep rounds short so a bad hand is just a quick chapter, not a slog.
wantItHarder: Randomise mid-life events too (a forced move, a surprise child).
Commit to a full lifetime on a single rolled hand, no fresh rounds.
variations: A "wheel of fate" version with a physical wheel for in-stream audience input. ---
credit: Original — Plumbob, a fully-randomised play format.
allowed: Use a random generator each round and adapt to whatever luck hands you; use `bb.moveobjects`.
notAllowed: Play only what you're given — no re-rolling results you dislike.

title: No-Pause Challenge
backstory: The pause button is your safety blanket — and it's gone. Complete a goal in real time, thinking on your feet while the clock keeps ticking, queueing actions on the fly with no time-outs to plan. Surprisingly intense.
bestFor: Players who want a real-time test
premise: The pause button is your safety blanket — and it's gone.
goal: Complete a defined goal **without ever pausing** the game.
gettingStarted: Pick a goal achievable in one session, then play it through start to finish without touching pause.
Choose a one-session goal.
Play at any speed — but **never pause**.
generalRules: The game is never paused from start to goal.
Speeding time up or down is allowed; stopping it is not.
If you pause, the run is void.
specificRules: Define a goal completable in a single unpaused sitting.
Queue and manage actions in real time.
Reach the goal without a single pause.
strategyTips: Queue several actions ahead so your Sim always has something to do while you think.
Use the slow-speed setting (not pause) when you need a moment to plan.
Pick a forgiving first goal — real-time micromanagement is harder than it sounds.
wantItHarder: No speeding up *or* slowing down either — real time only.
A multi-step goal that demands constant queueing.
variations: A "no-pause day" extending the rule across a full in-game day. ---
credit: Original — Plumbob, a real-time constraint format.
allowed: Play at any speed and plan ahead; set up with `bb.moveobjects` before you start.
notAllowed: Never pause once started — and avoid menus that pause the game.
Complete the goal entirely live.
objectives: Finish the goal, never having paused
baseGameVersion: Fully base-game.

title: Five-Minute Makeover

bestFor: Builders and stylists who want speed reps
premise: Five real minutes on the clock.
goal: Re-style a Sim **and** their room against a **five-minute real-world timer**.
gettingStarted: Pick a Sim and one room, set a five-minute timer, and make over both before it ends.
generalRules: Both the Sim and the room are restyled within five minutes.
No pre-planning the makeover.
Whatever exists at 5:00 is final.
specificRules: Change the Sim's look (hair, outfit, makeup) and the room's furnishings.
Commit to choices fast — no undo spirals.
Stop the moment the timer ends.

Use "buy by the room" and full CAS outfit swaps to move fast.
Done-and-bold beats half-finished-and-safe when the buzzer goes.
wantItHarder: Three minutes instead of five.
A random theme revealed only when the timer starts.
variations: A head-to-head "makeover battle" judged at the end. ---

notAllowed: No pre-planning before the timer starts.

title: The Streak
backstory: One small goal, every day, for two weeks — without missing once. It's not about any single day being hard; it's about showing up fourteen times in a row. Consistency is the whole challenge.
bestFor: Players who want a consistency habit
premise: One small goal, every day, for two weeks — without missing once.
goal: Complete a **daily mini-goal every day for a fortnight** without missing once.
gettingStarted: Choose one repeatable daily goal (paint a picture, make a friend, earn §300), then hit it every day for fourteen days.
generalRules: The same (or a rotating) mini-goal is completed every in-game day.
Missing a single day breaks the streak — restart from zero.
Fourteen unbroken days wins.
specificRules: The daily goal is small and genuinely repeatable.
It must be done fresh each day, not banked ahead.
Track the streak honestly.
strategyTips: Pick a goal that fits a normal day so life events don't force a miss.
Do the goal early each day so a late-night surprise can't break the streak.
If it feels too easy by day five, that's fine — the test is the fourteen, not the one.
wantItHarder: A 30-day streak.

title: Bingo Board
backstory: A five-by-five grid of random Sim tasks, and a simple goal: complete a full line — across, down, or diagonal — before anything else. Flexible, replayable, and as quick or as sprawling as you make it.
bestFor: Players who like a flexible checklist game
premise: A five-by-five grid of random Sim tasks, and a simple goal: complete a full line — across, down, or diagonal — before anything else.

Complete tasks in any order toward a line.
First full line ends the round.

Spot the line with the tasks you're closest to and steer toward it.
Keep boards saved — they're endlessly reusable and great for sharing.

title: The Gauntlet
backstory: Five mini-challenges, back to back, each harder than the last, all in one sitting. No breaks, no save-scumming — just a rising wall of tasks and your Sim's stamina against it. Endurance, distilled.
bestFor: Players who want an escalating endurance test
premise: Five mini-challenges, back to back, each harder than the last, all in one sitting.

Finishing all stages in one sitting is the win.

Failing a stage ends the gauntlet.

Design the final stage to be a genuine wall, so finishing feels earned.
wantItHarder: Seven stages instead of five.
A single shared time limit across the entire gauntlet.
variations: A community gauntlet where everyone runs the same five stages for a best time. ---

allowed: Line up a fixed sequence of escalating mini-challenges; play them in one sitting; use `bb.moveobjects`.

title: Pack Legacy
backstory: Your DLC shelf, turned into a family tree. Each generation lives and breathes a single pack — a city-living heir, a spellcaster heir, a horse-ranch heir — played in the order the packs released, until your whole collection has had its moment in the sun.

One heir carries the line to the next pack.

No skipping ahead — packs are played in order.

title: The Single-Pack Challenge

premise: Strip it back to one.

Base-game systems are fine; other DLC is set aside.

Complete the pack's headline feature.
Keep the run focused on that one pack.
strategyTips: Pick a pack with a strong central system (Vampires, Realm of Magic, Island Living) for the richest single-pack run.
Read up on everything the pack adds first, so you don't miss its hidden features.
Treat it as a tour — the goal is to see *all* of one pack, not rush it.
wantItHarder: 100% the pack — every aspiration, collectible and mechanic it introduces.

notAllowed: Don't use features from any other pack you own.

premise: Be honest — there are features in your library you've literally never touched.
goal: Make **every session use a mechanic you've never actually touched**, until your whole library has been used.

generalRules: Each session features at least one never-before-used mechanic.

Continue until your unused-features list is empty.
specificRules: Make a list of mechanics you've never tried.
Cross one off per session by actually using it.
No repeating until the list is exhausted.

Build a Sim whose life can plausibly touch many systems, so new mechanics fit naturally.
Keep the list visible; ticking it off is half the satisfaction.
wantItHarder: Use a new mechanic *every in-game day*, not just every session.
Build a whole Sim's life story out of the features you'd avoided.
milestones: Whole library used at least once
variations: A "reverse" version focusing only on your single most-neglected pack. ---

notAllowed: No repeating a mechanic until every one has been used.
Genuinely engage each mechanic — no token use.
objectives: Tackle the mechanic you've most avoided

Complete the new pack's headline feature with them.
Keep all the lives in one evolving save.

title: Kit Collector
backstory: Tiny packs, big discipline. Design a room or a Sim's whole look using just one Build or CAS kit at a time — nothing else. The narrowest palette in the game, and a brilliant way to finally use those kits gathering dust.

premise: Tiny packs, big discipline.

gettingStarted: Pick one kit and create a room (Build kit) or a complete outfit (CAS kit) using essentially only its contents.

One kit per creation.

Keep off-kit items to a bare minimum.
Work through your kits one at a time.

wantItHarder: Use *only* the kit — no base-game items at all, structure included where possible.

generalRules: The run is built around the chosen neglected pack.
Its features form the backbone of play.
Give the pack a genuine, full showcase.
specificRules: Use the pack's world, mechanics and aspirations as the run's core.
Complete its headline content.
No leaning on your favourite packs to carry the run.
strategyTips: Be honest about which pack you've truly ignored — that's the one with the most to discover.
Approach it fresh: read what it actually adds, since you clearly never found out before.
Commit fully; the magic is realising a "boring" pack was great all along.
wantItHarder: 100% the forgotten pack.
Do a run for each of your three least-used packs in turn.
variations: A "redemption tour" working through every pack you've barely touched. ---
credit: Original — Plumbob, an underused-pack format.
allowed: Use your least-touched pack only (plus base game); use `bb.moveobjects`.
notAllowed: Build the whole challenge around that one neglected pack.
Don't drift back to your comfort packs.
objectives: Build a Sim and home around it
Finish a full challenge on the forgotten pack
baseGameVersion: Needs at least one underused pack; base-game players instead pick an in-game system they've always ignored.

title: World Hopper
backstory: Childhood in one world, teen years in another, adulthood somewhere else again — a single Sim lives each life stage in a different corner of the map before finally choosing the one place to call home for good.
bestFor: Players who want one Sim to see the whole map
premise: Childhood in one world, teen years in another, adulthood somewhere else again — a single Sim lives each life stage in a different corner of the map before finally choosing the one place to call home for good.
goal: Live **one full life stage in every world** before finally settling down.
gettingStarted: Assign a world to each life stage and move your Sim on every birthday until they pick a forever home.

setup.label: Lot
setup.value: A new world each life stage

generalRules: Each life stage is spent in a different world.
The Sim moves on every age-up.
They settle permanently only at the end.
specificRules: Spend the whole life stage genuinely living in that world.
Engage each world's local life before moving.
The final world is chosen as a true "home."
strategyTips: Match worlds to life stages thematically — a lively city for the young-adult years, a calm rural world for elderhood.
Keep income portable so each move is affordable.
Treat the final choice as the story's payoff — which world did this Sim's whole life lead them home to?
wantItHarder: Make a lasting friend in every world before moving on.
Visit *every* world you own across the single lifetime, not just one per stage.
variations: A family version where each child is raised in a different world. ---
credit: Original — Plumbob, a world-hopping life format.
allowed: Live one full life stage in each world, then move; do each world's flavour; use `bb.moveobjects`.
notAllowed: One full life stage per world before settling down.
Move on schedule and don't repeat a world.
objectives: Spend childhood in the first world
Move to a new world each life stage
Settle down after touring them all
baseGameVersion: The base game's two-to-three worlds make a short hop; the run scales up with every pack's world.

title: Game Pack Gauntlet
backstory: The mid-size packs, back to back. Vampires, then Realm of Magic, then Parenthood, then Jungle Adventure — one after another you clear the signature challenge of each game pack you own, a themed tour through the game's most characterful chunks.
bestFor: Players who own several game packs
premise: The mid-size packs, back to back.
goal: Clear the **signature challenge of each game pack** you own, in sequence.
gettingStarted: List your game packs and their signature goals, then complete them one after another.

setup.label: Lot
setup.value: Themed to the game pack in play

generalRules: Each game pack's signature content is completed.
Tackle them in sequence.
Cover every game pack you own.
specificRules: Define each pack's signature goal (Vampires → Grand Master; Realm of Magic → Virtuoso; Parenthood → a perfectly-raised child; Jungle Adventure → conquer Selvadorada's temple; Dine Out → a five-star restaurant; and so on).
Complete each before the next.
Clear them all.
strategyTips: Group the occult packs together — vampire, spellcaster and werewolf goals share a play style.
Some signature goals are quick (Jungle Adventure's temple); others are long (a perfectly-parented child) — alternate to keep momentum.
A relay of Sims spreads the gauntlet across a family if needed.
wantItHarder: One Sim clears every game pack's signature goal in a single lifetime.
Add each game pack's aspiration to the requirement.
variations: Pair with the Expansion Marathon for a complete "beat every pack" mega-run. ---
credit: Original — Plumbob, a game-pack completion format.
allowed: Clear each game pack's signature challenge in sequence; use `bb.moveobjects`.
notAllowed: Go in order — clear one before the next.
Don't skip an owned game pack.
objectives: List your game packs' signature challenges
Complete every owned game pack's challenge
baseGameVersion: Requires game packs by definition — base-game-only players have none to clear here.

title: Seasons of Change
backstory: A full year lived at the mercy of the weather. Blistering summers, freezing winters, the rhythm of holidays and harvests — this run leans all the way into Seasons, letting the calendar itself drive every decision your Sim makes.
bestFor: Players who want weather to drive the story
premise: A full year lived at the mercy of the weather.
goal: Play a **year-long run that leans hard into every season's mechanics**, letting weather drive the whole story.
gettingStarted: Create a Sim, set the year to start in spring, and let each season genuinely dictate life.

setup.label: Lot
setup.value: A home that must weather all four seasons

generalRules: Each season meaningfully changes how the Sim lives.
Weather, temperature and holidays are embraced, not ignored.
The run spans a full year, spring to spring.
specificRules: Adapt activities, clothing and income to each season.
Celebrate the calendar's holidays as they come.
Survive summer's heat and winter's cold without simply hiding indoors.
strategyTips: Dress for the temperature and manage heat/cold moodlets — Seasons genuinely punishes ignoring them.
Build a life that shifts with the calendar: gardening in spring/summer, holidays in winter.
Lean on the holiday system; custom holidays let you theme the whole year.
wantItHarder: Go Off-the-Grid so heating and cooling are entirely self-managed through every extreme.
Survive a "harsh weather" run with longer, more severe seasons.
variations: A farming version (Cottage Living) where the whole economy follows the growing seasons. ---
credit: Original — Plumbob, built around the Seasons calendar and weather.
allowed: Use all of Seasons' weather, holidays and the calendar; let weather drive the story; use `bb.moveobjects`.
notAllowed: Play a full year through all four seasons.
Don't skip or cheat the weather — each season is meaningfully played.
objectives: Bring in a full autumn harvest
baseGameVersion: Requires Seasons — there's no base-game version.

title: The Grand Tour
backstory: A lifetime of holidays. Granite Falls, Selvadorada, Mt. Komorebi, Batuu — one family ticks off every getaway destination you own, turning a single Sim's life into the ultimate travel diary.
bestFor: Players who own the vacation packs
premise: A lifetime of holidays.
goal: **Vacation in every getaway destination you own** with one family.
gettingStarted: List your vacation worlds, then take your family on a trip to each over the course of their lives.
generalRules: The family visits every getaway world you own.
Each trip genuinely engages that destination's activities.
Continue until everywhere has been visited.
specificRules: Take a proper trip to each (not a flying visit).
Do each destination's signature activities (hike Granite Falls, explore Selvadorada's temple, ski Mt. Komorebi).
Cover every vacation spot you own.
strategyTips: Budget for travel — getaways cost money, so keep a steady home income between trips.
Do each destination's headline activity properly; that's the point of the tour.
Space the trips across the family's life so each feels like a real holiday, not a checklist sprint.
wantItHarder: Complete a destination-specific goal on every trip (the temple, a Granite Falls collection, a Batuu faction mission).
Visit every getaway within a single Sim's lifetime.
milestones: A signature activity in each so far
variations: A "honeymoon legacy" where each generation's couple visits a different getaway. ---
credit: Original — Plumbob, a vacation-destination tour format.
allowed: Vacation in each getaway you own (Granite Falls, Selvadorada, Sulani, Mt. Komorebi, Tartosa, Batuu) with one family.
Use `bb.moveobjects`.
notAllowed: Visit every owned destination and do each one's signature activity.
Keep it one continuous family story.
objectives: Vacation in every getaway you own
baseGameVersion: Needs vacation packs — the base game has no travel destinations, so there's no base-game version.

title: Everything Everywhere
backstory: The ultimate test of a full library. One sprawling mega-legacy that must meaningfully use every single pack you own over its run — every world, every occult, every career, every mechanic — a true grand finale that leaves no DLC untouched.
bestFor: Completionists with a big library and big patience
premise: The ultimate test of a full library.
goal: Run a **mega-legacy that meaningfully uses all of your packs** over its lifetime — the ultimate full-library test.
gettingStarted: Found a bloodline and commit to touching every world, occult, career, skill and mechanic you own before the run ends.
Keep a master checklist of every pack's headline content.

setup.label: Lot
setup.value: A founding home (the legacy can roam)

generalRules: Every owned pack must be meaningfully used before the run is "complete."
The legacy continues across as many generations as it takes.
No pack left untouched.
specificRules: Track each pack's headline content on a master list.
Different generations can specialise (an occult heir, a fame heir, a farming heir) to cover the library.
The run ends only when the whole list is ticked.
strategyTips: This is essentially every other Pack-Driven challenge rolled into one — borrow the Pack Legacy roadmap and the Expansion Marathon checklist.
Assign blocks of packs to generations so no single heir is overloaded.
Keep one master spreadsheet/list; with a big library, memory alone won't cut it.
wantItHarder: 100% every pack — every aspiration, collection, world and mechanic across the whole library.
Do it on a single founding lot, Legacy-style, never moving.
milestones: Down to the final few packs
variations: A "speed" version racing to touch every pack in the fewest generations. --- *That's all 130 challenges — ten categories, fully detailed. However you like to play The Sims 4, there's a goal here waiting for you. Now go forth, and may your plumbob always glow green.* — **Sir Plumbob**
credit: Original — Plumbob, the ultimate full-library legacy.
allowed: Run one continuous mega-legacy that uses all your packs meaningfully; use earned Potions of Youth and `bb.moveobjects`.
notAllowed: Every pack must be used meaningfully, not as a token.
Keep it one legacy — don't leave a pack untouched.
objectives: Map each pack to a generation or era
Make a strong start using several packs
Reach the halfway mark with most packs used
Finish a legacy that meaningfully used them all
baseGameVersion: The ultimate full-library test — base-game players simply run a standard legacy, and it scales entirely with what you own.

title: Very Veggie Legacy
backstory: lilsimsie's goofier sequel to Not So Berry: ten generations, each themed after a different vegetable, built rags-to-riches from a bare lot and §0 — one generation literally lives in an eggplant costume. It was written as a charity reward when her Twitch community raised over §600,000 for St. Jude.
bestFor: Players who loved Not So Berry and want the newer-pack, goofier follow-up
premise: Ten generations, each heir themed to a different vegetable with its own colour, traits, career, aspiration and goals — a colourful rags-to-riches legacy.
goal: Complete ten generations, each heir themed to a different vegetable with its own colour, traits, career, aspiration and goals — starting from nothing on an empty lot.
gettingStarted: Create one young adult Broccoli founder, themed in dark green.
Move them onto any empty lot.
Clear their funds to §0, then build up rags-to-riches.

setup.label: Lot
setup.value: Any empty lot (you may move lots between generations)

generalRules: Each generation is themed around its assigned vegetable — dress the Sim and decorate the home to match.
Start rags-to-riches: §0 on an empty lot, building up from nothing.
Complete a generation's goals before that heir dies; then choose an heir for the next.
specificRules: Each heir takes the assigned traits, career and aspiration for their vegetable.
Follow each generation's special goals (see the generation ladder).
The rules are flexible — swap anything for packs you don't own, and a base-game version is provided per generation.
strategyTips: Lean into the rags-to-riches engine early (gardening, a small business, selling crafts) — the Broccoli founder sets your whole financial foundation.
Keep a running note of each heir's traits, career and aspiration so you never drift off-theme.
Don't fear the silliness — the eggplant-costume generation is the most beloved part; commit to it.
wantItHarder: Forbid money cheats entirely, including the opening — earn your way out of §0 the hard way.
Stay on a single lot for all ten generations, classic-legacy style.
variations: Play a base-game-only run using the written base-game ruleset for every generation, or a single-lot version for a stricter classic-legacy feel.
scoring: There's no formal score; lilsimsie's stated goal is fun, goofy gameplay. Track completion generation by generation.
credit: Created by lilsimsie (Kayla Sims) in 2025 as a follow-up to Not So Berry (co-written with alwaysimming, 2017). Written as an incentive for her annual Twitch fundraiser after her community raised over §600,000 for St. Jude Children's Research Hospital; the eggplant generation was inspired by the Lovestruck costume. Full rules and base-game version on lilsimsie.com.

generationLadder.notes: Traits Creative, Art Lover, High Maintenance. Run a 5-star business, max Wellness, max three artistic skills.

Traits Horse Lover, Unflirty, Loner. Max Horse Riding, own three horses, grow carrots, don't marry until adulthood.
Traits Ambitious, Hates Children, Geek. Graduate with a B or higher, complete Academic and Master Mentor.
Traits Freegan, Nosy, Slob. Be a 5-star property owner with three occupied units, befriend a Dust Bunny.
Traits Socially Awkward, Hot-Headed, Overachiever. Grow tomatoes, max Gardening/Cooking/Gourmet, own an outdoor kitchen.
Traits Paranoid, Bookworm, Skeptical. Complete Soul's Journey, live in a haunted house, marry a ghost.
Traits Adventurous, Chased by Death, Active. Complete both adventure aspirations, visit space.
Traits Vegetarian, Loves the Outdoors, Cat/Dog Lover. Adopt a stray, grow every Cottage Living mushroom variety.
Traits Self-Absorbed, Materialistic, Music Lover. Become a 5-star celebrity, reach §250,000, max Piano/Violin/Guitar, don't cook.

allowed: Move to new lots and build new homes between generations (unlike a classic legacy).
Adjust or swap any rule for the packs you own; use the written base-game version for any generation.
Lean all the way into the theme — outfits, home, even your Sims' names.
notAllowed: The only cheat is the opening money 0 — avoid money and gameplay cheats after that.
Don't change a generation's assigned vegetable, traits, career or aspiration.
Don't skip a generation's goals before passing on the heirship.
objectives: Gen 1 Broccoli: run a 5-star business, max Wellness, max three artistic skills
Gen 2 Eggplant: wear the eggplant costume for every outfit; grow oversized eggplants
Gen 4 Squash: graduate university with a B or higher; complete Academic + Master Mentor
Gen 6 Tomato: grow tomatoes; max Gardening, Cooking and Gourmet Cooking
Gen 9 Mushroom: grow every Cottage Living mushroom variety
Gen 10 Pea: become a 5-star celebrity and reach a §250,000 net worth
baseGameVersion: A full base-game ruleset is written for every generation (for example, Broccoli maxes the Video Gaming skill plus three base-game artistic skills; Pea reaches §250,000 with maxed Piano, Violin and Guitar). Wherever a generation names a pack, substitute its base-game alternative from lilsimsie's spreadsheet.
